evidence.text: le Stromal cell subsets modulate T-cell infiltration in early breast cancer DOI 10.6019/S-BSST3074 ReleaseDate 2026-06-08 study-12-66ch Study Title Stromal cell subsets modulate

evidence_text: S-BSST3074 study Stromal cell subsets modulate T-cell infiltration in early breast cancer Julia Chen Hanyun Zhang Travis Ruan Sunny Z Wu Iveta Slapetova Ewan Millar Peter H Graham Jodi Lynch Lois H Browne Elgene Lim Alexander Swarbrick 3 2026-06-08 True  the differential impact of stromal subsets on immune infiltration and activation within the breast cancer tumour microenvironment with potential implications for patient outcomes. Homo sapiens (human) NCBITaxon NCBITaxon_9606 CC0 https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/legalcode Julia Chen S-BSST3074 Template Default.v2 Title Stromal cell subsets modulate T-cell infiltration in early breast cancer DOI 10.6019/S-BSST3074 ReleaseDate 2026-06-08 study-12-66ch Study Title Stromal cell subsets modulate T-cell infiltration in early breast cancer ReleaseDate 2026-06-08 Description Recent studies of the tumour microenvironment have elucidated the heterogeneity of stromal cells, with increasing evidence suggesting that stromal subsets play an important role in regulating anti-tumour immunity in breast cancer. However, the functional diversity of these cells within the tumour immune microenvironment and how they interact with immune cells in a spatial and clinical context remain poorly understood. We performed multiplex immunofluorescence on tumour microarrays from two cohorts consisting of 591 breast cancer patients to assess the abundance and spatial co-localisation of stromal and immune subsets and their correlation with clinicopathological features and patient outcomes. We found that stromal cells were spatially distinct. A perivascular-like subset that was disseminated throughout the stroma rather than restricted to vessel-adjacent regions was enriched in an immune cold environment and associated with T-cell exclusion. An inflammatory-like cancer-associated fibroblast subset was associated with segregation of T cells from cancer cells. Our findings highlight the differential impact of stromal subsets on immune infiltration and activation within the breast cancer tumour microenvironment with potential implications for patient outcomes. sex: male and female Fresh-frozen spleen sections prepared for 10x Xenium Fresh-frozen spleens (snap-frozen in OCT using a PrestoCHILL S-BIAD3359 Template BioImages.v5 Title SpatialBench (IST): Comparative benchmarking of high-resolution spatial transcriptomics platforms using immune reference tissue DOI 10.6019/S-BIAD3359 ReleaseDate 2026-06-15 AttachTo BioImages study-0-qsvz Study Title SpatialBench (IST): Comparative benchmarking of high-resolution spatial transcriptomics platforms using immune reference tissue ReleaseDate 2026-06-15 Description The SpatialBench dataset comprises spatial transcriptomics data generated for cross-platform benchmarking of sequencing-based and imaging-based platforms. The study uses fresh-frozen mouse (Mus musculus,  C57/Bl6) spleen tissue from wild-type, control, and knock-out samples, providing a spatially organized immunological tissue with defined structural and transcriptomic differences between conditions. Detailed animal model information provided below. A total of 27 spatial datasets were generated across multiple experimental batches, including 14 from the 10x Genomics Xenium platform (9 unimodal and 5 Multimodal),  9 from Vizgen MERSCOPE, and 4 from the whole transcriptome 10x Genomics Visium HD platform. Xenium and MERSCOPE used targeted gene panels informed by previous SpatialBench Visium dataset (Du, et. al. 2025), including genes relevant to germinal center biology.

This accession includes the 10x 

Mouse Model:
Mice are a C57/Bl6 Tbx21^fl/flCd23^cre (Knock-Out) strain. Both male and female mice were used for the experiments. Eight to 12-week-old mice were infected intravenously with 5x10^4 P.berghei ANKA pRBCs. Mice were treated with a dose chloroquine (10 mg/kg) and pyrimethamine (10 mg/kg) intraperitoneally at the onset of severe malaria symptoms on day 5 post infection, followed by treatment with drinking water containing chloroquine and pyrimethamine (10 mg/kg) for 7 days. sex: male and female spec-5-74fh Specimen Title Fresh-frozen spleen sections prepared for 10x Xenium Sample preparation protocol Fresh-frozen spleens (snap-frozen in OCT using a PrestoCHILL, Milestone) were cryosectioned at 10 µm on a CryoStar NX70 cryostat (Epredia) onto Xenium slides (10x Genomics, PN-1000460) and stored at −80 °C for ≤4 weeks. Slides were prepared following the Xenium In Situ for Fresh Frozen Tissues protocol (CG000579, Rev F): incubated at 37 °C, fixed in formaldehyde, permeabilised with methanol, and assembled into Xenium cassettes. Probe hybridisation, ligation and amplification followed 10x Genomics protocol CG000582, Rev H, using a custom 100-gene mouse panel designed for splenic immune cell-type identification and malaria-associated inflammation biology (gene list in Supplemental Table 2 / linked GitHub repository). Selected samples (CTRL172, CTRL173, CTRL174, WT709, WT710) were additionally stained overnight with the Xenium Cell Segmentation Add-on Kit (PN-1000662; protocol CG000749, Rev B), which labels nuclei (DAPI), cell membranes (ATP1A1, E-Cadherin, CD45), cytoplasm (18S rRNA) and cytoskeleton (αSMA/Vimentin) across four channels. spec-1-1s89-removable Specimen Title Fresh-frozen mouse spleen sections prepared for Vizgen MERSCOPE Sample preparation protocol Fresh-frozen spleens were cryosectioned at 10 µm onto MERSCOPE slides (Vizgen, 1050001) using a Feather S35 blade and CryoStar NX70 cryostat (Epredia). Sections were held in the cryostat for 15 min, transferred to a 37 °C hot plate for 1 min to improve adherence, fixed in freshly prepared 4% PFA/PBS, and permeabilised in 70% ethanol overnight at 4 °C. Autofluorescence was quenched in a Photobleacher instrument (Vizgen, 1010003) for ≥4 h at room temperature; slides were then stored ≤4 weeks at 4 °C. Cell boundaries were stained with the Vizgen Cell Boundary Kit (10400118), after which encoding-probe hybridisation was carried out for 36 h at 37 °C in a humidified incubator. Sections were embedded in a thin polyacrylamide gel for RNA anchoring and cleared with proteinase K (NEB P8107S, 1:100) for 2–4 h at 37 °C until no visible tissue remained. imag-6-ovdl Image acquisition Title Xenium Analyser in situ imaging of mouse spleen Imaging instrument 10x Genomics Xenium Analyser Image acquisition parameters Imaging was performed on the 10x Genomics Xenium Analyser following the Xenium Analyser User Guide (CG000584, Rev K). Data were acquired in iterative cycles of fluorescent probe hybridisation, image capture and probe stripping under automated fluidic control, with the codebook matched to the 100-gene panel. The Xenium Onboard Analysis pipeline performed image processing, registration, segmentation, decoding, deduplication and secondary analysis on-instrument. Samples were acquired across three batches on different software/pipeline versions: batch 1 (WT713, CTRL173-rep1, KO166, KO167) — instrument v1.7.6.0, Onboard Analysis v1.7.1.0; batch 2 (WT709-rep1, WT710-rep1, CTRL172, CTRL174-rep1, KO168) — instrument v1.8.2.1, Onboard Analysis v1.7.1.0; batch 3 (WT709-rep2, WT710-rep2, CTRL172-rep2, CTRL173-rep2, CTRL174-rep2) — instrument v3.1.0.0, Onboard Analysis v3.1.0.4. After the run, slides were de-quenched (CG000613, Rev B) and stained with Mayer's H&E; histology images were captured on an Olympus SLIDEVIEW VS200 slide scanner. Imaging method in situ hybridisation imaging Imaging method Xenium imag-2-rv1t-removable Image acquisition Title MERSCOPE MERFISH imaging of mouse spleen Imaging instrument Vizgen MERSCOPE Image acquisition parameters MERFISH imaging was performed on the Vizgen MERSCOPE following the MERSCOPE Instrument User Guide (91600001). An imaging kit was activated and loaded; slides were stained with DAPI and Poly(T) reagent, assembled into the flow chamber and connected to the instrument fluidics. A low-resolution 10× image of DAPI and Poly(T) was acquired and regions of interest drawn in the MERSCOPE software. Imaging then used a 60×/1.4 NA oil-immersion objective with automated fluidics for iterative rounds of readout-probe hybridisation and removal. Images were collected as eight 1.5 µm z-stacks spanning 12 µm total thickness. Readout probes were imaged in the 560, 650 and 750 nm channels; DAPI and Poly(T) in the 405 and 488 nm channels. To image the 91-gene panel plus cell-boundary stains, each run comprised six rounds of imaging for an 18-bit MERFISH experiment, followed by three sequential rounds of three-colour FISH. Total MERFISH imaging time was ~24 h per experiment. Samples were acquired on two MERSCOPE software versions: 232.230125.316b (Ctrl174a, KO166b) and 233.230615.567 (Ctrl172, Ctrl173, Ctrl174, KO167, KO168, WT709, WT710, WT713). Imaging method MERSCOPE Imaging method  in situ hybridization imaging stud-10-a4pu Study Component Name Xenium Description 14 Xenium (10x Genomics) in situ spatial transcriptomics datasets from fresh-frozen mouse spleen across wild-type, control and B-cell-specific Tbx21 knock-out genotypes, profiled with a 100-gene targeted panel. Includes 9 unimodal and 5 multimodal (cell-segmentation-stained) acquisitions across three instrument software versions. Each dataset is a Xenium Onboard Analysis output directory. Tissues were section in 10 µm slices and four samples were placed on one Xenium slide. Subsequently, the tissue was fixed with PFA according to the manufacture´s protocol. Tissues were permeabilized with SDS, incubated in 70% ice cold methanol and washed with PBS. Hybridization of the human generic brain panel with 70 add-on genes (Supplementary Dataset 1) was performed at 50°C in a Bio-Rad C1000 touch cycler for 20 hours. Washing, ligation and amplification steps were carried out according to the manufacturer’s instructions. ROIs were selected according to the tissue area excluding non-tissue covered tiles.  Each transcript was imaged in a bright state five times across 60 cycle-channels (15 cycles x 4 channels). After the run on the Xenium analyzer slides were removed and buffer exchanged with PBS-T for further storage at 4°C. Hybridization of the human generic brain panel with 70 add-on genes (Supplementary Dataset 1) was performed at 50°C in a Bio-Rad C1000 touch cycler for 20 hours. Washing, ligation and amplification steps were carried out according to the manufacturer’s instructions. ROIs were selected according to the tissue area excluding non-tissue covered tiles.  Each transcript was imaged in a bright state five times across 60 cycle-channels (15 cycles x 4 channels). After the run on the Xenium analyzer slides were removed and buffer exchanged with PBS-T for further storage at 4°C. Specimen-13 Specimen Title MB299_Xenium Sample preparation protocol 10 µm cryosections were acquired using the cryostar NX50 with a cutting temperature of -15 °C. Tissues were section in 10 µm slices and four samples were placed on one Xenium slide. Subsequently, the tissue was fixed with PFA according to the manufacture´s protocol. Tissues were permeabilized with SDS, incubated in 70% ice cold methanol and washed with PBS. Hybridization of the human generic brain panel with 70 add-on genes (Supplementary Dataset 1) was performed at 50°C in a Bio-Rad C1000 touch cycler for 20 hours. Washing, ligation and amplification steps were carried out according to the manufacturer’s instructions. ROIs were selected according to the tissue area excluding non-tissue covered tiles.  Each transcript was imaged in a bright state five times across 60 cycle-channels (15 cycles x 4 channels). After the run on the Xenium analyzer slides were removed and buffer exchanged with PBS-T for further storage at 4°C. Specimen-16 Specimen Title MB295_Merscope Sample preparation protocol Tissues were sectioned in 10 µm slices and placed on one Merscope slide. Subsequently, the tissue was fixed with 4% PFA at 37°C for 15 min. After washing the with PBS, the sections were permeabilized with 70% ethanol at 4°C and until the hybridization was started. The panel was hybridized for 48 hours, and all steps were performed according to the manufactures protocol including the membrane staining.
Gene specific probes and amplification oligonucleotides were tested with the protein verification kit provided by Vizgen for the Merscope. A list of primary, secondary and amplification probes can be found in Supplementary Table S6. The tissue was fixed and permeabilized as described above, washed with 30% formamide in 2x SSC (wash buffer) and incubated with the primary probes at 1 µM concentration in hybridization buffer (0.05 % yeast rRNA, 1U/µl RNase inhibitor, 30% formamide, 2x SSC, 10% dextran). After 36 h of incubation at 37 °C in a humid environment the tissue was washed three times with wash buffer at 47 °C. The tissue was embedded according to the manufacturer’s instructions and incubated in clearing solution for 24h. Then, the tissue was washed with amplification buffer (10% formamide, 2x SSC) and the primary amplifier was hybridized at 5 nM in hybridization buffer (0.05 % yeast rRNA, 1U/µl RNase inhibitor, 10% formamide, 2x SSC, 10% dextran) for 30 min at 37°C. After three washes with amplification buffer the secondary amp probe was hybridized at 5 nM concentration in amp hybridization buffer (0.05 % yeast rRNA, 1U/µl RNase inhibitor, 10% formamide, 2x SSC, 10% dextran) for 30 min at 37 °C. After three washes the verification reagent was added for 15 min followed by a formamide and sample prep wash. The readout of the amplification probe was done with the protein verification kit (mouse, rabbit, goat) using only the mouse and rabbit channels. Specimen-17 Specimen Title MB266_Merscope Sample preparation protocol Tissues were sectioned in 10 µm slices and placed on one Merscope slide. Subsequently, the tissue was fixed with 4% PFA at 37°C for 15 min. After washing the with PBS, the sections were permeabilized with 70% ethanol at 4°C and until the hybridization was started. Specimen-22 Specimen Title MB299_COMET_Slide Sample preparation protocol After completion of the Xenium run, the slides were washed twice with PBS and then placed in the comet system. The immuno-oncology SPYRE panel was used to stain and image the tissue section of the sample MB299 using the standard SPYRE protocol.  Image acquisition-3 Image acquisition Title Reimaging Imaging instrument Andor Dragonfly 505 spinning disc confocal system equipped with a Nikon Ti2-E inverted microscope and a CFI P-Fluor 40X/1.30 oil objective or a Plan Apo 60x/1.40 oil objective. Image acquisition parameters Multicolor images were acquired with the following laser lines 405 nm (DAPI), 488 nm (Alexa 488, eosin), 561 nm (Atto 550), 637 nm (Atto 647) 730nm (Alexa 750). Images were recorded at 16-bit depth and with 1024x1024 pixels dimensions (pixel size: 0.301 µm or 0.217 µm) using an iXon Ultra 888 EM-CCD camera. The region of interest was selected based on the DAPI signal and 50 z-slices were acquired with a step size of 0.4 µm (20 µm z-range) per field of view (FOV). Tiles were imaged with a 10% overlap to ensure accurate stitching. Imaging method spinning disk confocal microscopy Image acquisition-14 Image acquisition Title Xenium spatial transcriptomics Imaging instrument Xenium Analyzer Image acquisition parameters Each transcript was imaged in a bright state five times across 60 cycle-channels (15 cycles x 4 channels). Washing buffer: MSB Imaging method COMET Image analysis-5 Image analysis Title Maximum Intensity Projection, Flat Fied and Chromatic Aberration Correction and Stitching Image analysis overview Widefield images from the MC and Xenium platform were integrated with reimaged SDCM data with the following workflow in ImageJ. First, SDCM image stacks were subjected to a maximum intensity projection, followed by flat field and chromatic aberration correction using a custom script. Next, image tiles were stitched using the “Grid/Collection Stitching” plugin. Image analysis-13 Image analysis Title Xenium image processing Image analysis overview Xenium Onboard Analysis performed with default parameters. Image analysis-14 Image analysis Title Registration Image analysis overview After stitching, the DAPI images from SDCM were registered to Xenium widefield images using “Register Virtual Stack Slices” with Affine feature extraction model and the Elastic bUnwarpJ splines registration model. In case of further staining, images were transformed via Transform Virtual Stack slices employing the transformation file of the DAPI registration. Image analysis-19 Image analysis Title Merscope_Analysis Image analysis overview The MERSCOPE Instrument Software (in combination with the MERSCOPE Analysis Computer) automatically processes the raw images to output spatial genomics measurements in a format ready for immediate downstream analysis. The output includes the list of all detected transcripts and their spatial locations in three dimensions (CSV files), the mosaic images (TIFF), output from the cell segmentation analysis: the transcripts per cell matrix (CSV), the cell metadata (CSV), the cell boundaries (HDF5), and a binary for use with the MERSCOPE Vizualizer software. evidence_text: S-BIAD1426 study An integrative spatial multi-omic workflow for unified analysis of tumor tissue 2 1474 2024-12-28 135 True  Institute of Medical Research https://ror.org/01b6kha49 An integrative spatial multi-omic workflow for unified analysis of tumor tissue Jurgen Kriel, Joel J.D. Moffet, Tianyao Lu 2024 Human Low Grade Glioma (WHO Grade 3) Homo sapiens (human) Human Low Grade Glioma (WHO Grade 3) Human Low Grade Glioma S-BIAD1426 Template BioImages.v4 DOI 10.6019/S-BIAD1426 ReleaseDate 2024-12-28 AttachTo BioImages Study Title An integrative spatial multi-omic workflow for unified analysis of tumor tissue Description Combining molecular profiling with imaging techniques has advanced the field of spatial biology offering new insights into complex biological processes. Focusing on diffuse IDH-mutated low-grade glioma this study presents a workflow for Spatial Multi-omics Integration SMINT specifically combining spatial transcriptomics and spatial metabolomics. Our workflow incorporates both existing and custom-developed computational tools to enable cell segmentation and registration of spatial coordinates from both modalities to a common coordinate framework. During our investigation of cell segmentation strategies we found that nuclei-only segmentation while containing only 40% of segmented cell transcripts enables accurate cell type annotation but does not account for multinucleated cells. Our integrative workflow including cell-morphology segmentation identified distinct cellular neighborhoods at the infiltrating edge of gliomas which were enriched in multinucleated and oligodendrocyte-lineage tumor cells that may drive tumor invasion into the normal cortical layers of the brain. Moffet, Tianyao Lu Year 2024 Biosample-1 Biosample Title Human Low Grade Glioma (WHO Grade 3) Organism Homo sapiens (human) Biological entity Human Low Grade Glioma (WHO Grade 3) Specimen-2 Specimen Title Human Low Grade Glioma (WHO Grade 3) Sample preparation protocol Patient samples were obtained directly from surgery via the Royal Melbourne Hospital Neurosurgery Unit and were immediately flash-frozen directly in isopentane in a liquid nitrogen bath. Frozen tissue was stored at − 80 °C until downstream sample preparation. Frozen tissues were sectioned using a Cryostar NX70 at -20 °C. Briefly Image acquisition-3 Image acquisition Title Confocal Acquisition Imaging instrument Ziess LSM-980 Image acquisition parameters Stage calibration The LSM 980 confocal stage was calibrated to recognize the Xenium slide as a conventional 76 x 26mm slide. This was achieved by setting support points on each corner of the Xenium sample area. Set regions of interest and support points: A low resolution TPMT tilescan (256x256 pixels) of the entire sample area was taken at 10x magnification. Regions of interest were drawn accordingly for each section on the sample area. To ensure all tiles were captured at adequate focus 10-20 support points were added to each section depending on their size. Setting imaging parameters: Using a 20x objective the appropriate strength of 405 488 and 647 laser lines was determined. Thereafter the z-position of each support point was calibrated manually opting for a z-slice in the middle of the section. Tile sizes were set to 3852 x 3852 with a LSM scan speed of 7 at bidirectional direction. Small z-stacks of 5-6 slices were selected to reduce imaging time. After determining the upper and lower z-limit the center slice was determined and the z-stack strategy set to center for tile scan acquisition. Image processing and export: Orthogonal projections were first created using Zen Blue software (version 3.8). Thereafter individual tiles were exported as tiffs followed by stitching of the orthogonal projection in Zen Blue at 10% overlap. Resulting cell masks after segmentation were overlaid with the stitched output. confocal microscopy Imaging method Image correlation Image correlation-4 Title Alignment Fiducials used Markers were assigned based on anatomical regions Spatial and temporal alignment After manual landmark annotation, STalign was used to align spatial transcriptomics and metabolomics data based on the diffeomorphic metric mapping. Image analysis Image analysis-5 Title Segmentation Image analysis overview Pre-processing of images required merging of the 488 and 647 channels (FIJI/ImageJ version 1.54f) to ensure that cell outlines of GFAP negative cells would still be identified. 50 tiles from various regions of the original tile scan were then imported into Cellpose (version 2.0) and an initial segmentation run was conducted using the pretrained Cellpose model. The settings were as following Flow Threshold: 1 CellProbability Threshold: 0.4 Cell Diameter: 100 Channel to segment: 1 (the merged 488 and 647 images) Additional Channel to segment: 2 (405 Dapi channel). These initial masks were then assessed on a per tile basis with inaccurate segmentations deleted and manually redrawn for each cell. The segmentation files were then exported as seg.npy files for training. Training of the Cellpose model was run on a Nvidia A30 GPU at a learning_rate of 0.1 with a weight_decay of 0.0001 and 1000 epochs. Segmentations were run on all exported tiles using the Cellpose CLI segmentation function. For final segmentation the cellpose flow_threshold was set to 2.0 cellprob_threshold to -1 and diameter to 100.5. Outline coordinates were then saved as a text file for subsequent stitching in R along with seg.npy files for viewing. The pretrained Cellpose nuclei model was used for nuclei-only segmentation. The instrument is equipped with a Laser-diode-excited Nd:YAG laser and an atmospheric pressure MALDI. Data was acquired using a laser intensity at 60 for 10 µm spatial resolution detector voltage was set at 2.36 kV laser repetition frequency set at 1000 Hz desolvation line temperature maintained at 250 °C and a laser irradiation count of 50 shots were accumulated per pixel.   Imaging method Imaging Mass Spectrometry Study Component-12 Study Component Name Processed data Description Processed spatial transcriptomics and segmentation files File List processed_data.json asso-12 Associations Biosample Human Low Grade Glioma (WHO Grade 3) Specimen Human Low Grade Glioma (WHO Grade 3) Image acquisition Confocal Acquisition submission

evidence_text: S-BIAD1900 study Systematic Benchmarking of High-Throughput Subcellular Spatial Transcriptomics Platforms 36 2025-05-05 1 True  analyst o1 Zexian Zeng zexianzeng@pku.edu.cn corresponding author o1 Peking University HCC Homo sapiens (human) Moderately differentiated hepatocellular carcinoma Hepatocellular carcinoma OV Homo sapiens (human) High-grade serous carcinoma with necrosis in both ovaries Ovarian cancer COAD Homo S-BIAD1900 Template BioImages.v4 DOI 10.6019/S-BIAD1900 ReleaseDate 2025-05-05 AttachTo BioImages Study Title Systematic Benchmarking of High-Throughput Subcellular Spatial Transcriptomics Platforms Description We benchmarked five advanced spatial transcriptomics (ST) platforms: Stereo-seq v1.3, Visium HD FF, Visium HD FFPE, CosMx 6K, and Xenium 5K. These platforms were selected for their high-throughput gene capture (>5,000 genes), subcellular resolution (<=2um), and commercial availability. Samples from three treatment-naive tumors (colon adenocarcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, and ovarian cancer) were processed into FFPE blocks, fresh frozen blocks with OCT compound, and single-cell suspensions. Serial tissue sections were generated for consistent evaluation. For ground truth datasets, we profiled proteins from adjacent sections using CODEX and performed scRNA-seq on the same samples, enabling integrative cross-modal comparisons. Note that Visium HD FF protocol became available three months after initial sample preparation, possibly resulting in RNA degradation. Keywords spatial transcriptomics Keywords solid tumor study Acknowledgements We thank the Optical Imaging Core Facility, National Center for Protein Sciences at Peking University in Beijing, China, for assistance with the CODEX experiment. Torsten Söderberg Foundation Peter Lönnerberg peter.lonnerberg@ki.se submitter 0009-0009-0965-2906 o1 Sten Linnarsson sten.linnarsson@ki.se corresponding author o1 null Karolinska Institutet SL007 Homo sapiens (human) Left S-BIAD1700 Template BioImages.v4 Title 10Xgenomics Xenium analysis on glioblastoma organoids subject to in vitro perturbations with hypoxia and blood plasma. DOI 10.6019/S-BIAD1700 ReleaseDate 2025-09-01 AttachTo BioImages Study Title 10Xgenomics Xenium analysis on glioblastoma organoids subject to in vitro perturbations with hypoxia and blood plasma. ReleaseDate 2025-09-01 Description We performed in vitro perturbations of glioblastoma organoids (GBOs) derived from four glioblastoma patients in order to study wound response (WR) transcriptional states. Given the expression of hypoxia and hemostasis-related genes in WR states, we selected hypoxia and blood plasma as two candidates for perturbations. We subjected GBOs to either condition, or a combination of both, for 72 hours to test the inducibility of WR state, and reverted to regular media and normoxia for another 72 hours to assess the reversibility of WR state activation, performing Xenium spatial transcriptomics analysis at several timepoints.

 Biosample-9 Biosample Title SL014 Organism Homo sapiens (human) Biological entity Left frontal lobe glioblastoma Experimental variable Time Extrinsic variable Hypoxia and/or blood plasma treatment Biosample-10 Biosample Title SL027A Organism Homo sapiens (human) Biological entity Left frontal lobe glioblastoma Experimental variable Time Extrinsic variable Hypoxia and/or blood plasma treatment Specimen-2 Specimen Title SL007 organoid at 0h Sample preparation protocol Organoids were washed with 1× PBS (Thermo Fisher Scientific), fixed in 4% formaldehyde
(Thermo Fisher Scientific) in 1× PBS for 10 minutes and washed again with 1× PBS three
times. Before freezing, organoids were cryoprotected by allowing to sink in 15% and then 30%
sucrose (Sigma Aldrich) solutions in 1× PBS. Xenium (v1 platform, Human Brain panel with
custom add-on genes; 10x Genomics; Supplementary Table 4) experiments were performed by
the In Situ Sequencing Infrastructure Unit, SciLifeLab Stockholm. Growth protocol Organoids were grown as described in Jacob et al. (2020), 
biobanked in liquid nitrogen at passage 0-1, revived and grown to passage 3-
4. Organoids of homogenous size and shape were selected for experiment and grown in 4 ml
medium per condition in 6-well plates with continuous shaking. For blood plasma condition (pla),
medium containing 10% human plasma (Merck) was prepared by decreasing the volume of
DMEM/F12 and Neurobasal medium, while the concentrations of supplements were identical
to the standard medium. For hypoxia condition (hyp), organoids were grown in moderate hypoxia
(5% O2, 5% CO2) for the first 24 h and severe hypoxia (2% O2, 5% CO2) from timepoint 24 h to 72 h.
to 72 h. Specimen-11 Specimen Title SL007 organoid +hypoxia +plasma at 24h Sample preparation protocol Organoids were washed with 1× PBS (Thermo Fisher Scientific), fixed in 4% formaldehyde

4. Organoids of homogenous size and shape were selected for experiment and grown in 4 ml
medium per condition in 6-well plates with continuous shaking. For blood plasma condition (pla),
medium containing 10% human plasma (Merck) was prepared by decreasing the volume of
DMEM/F12 and Neurobasal medium, while the concentrations of supplements were identical
to the standard medium. For hypoxia condition (hyp), organoids were grown in moderate hypoxia
(5% O2, 5% CO2) for the first 24 h and severe hypoxia (2% O2, 5% CO2) from timepoint 24 h to 72 h.

— recurrent hepatocellular carcinoma (FFPE, liver) Homo sapiens (human) Sample S-BIAD2339 Template BioImages.v5 Title Spatial in situ transcriptomics of post-transplant
ost-transplant HCC recurrence using 10x Genomics Xenium												 DOI 10.6019/S-BIAD2339 ReleaseDate 2026-04-01 AttachTo BioImages study-0-pr5o Study Title Spatial in s
ics of post-transplant HCC recurrence using 10x Genomics Xenium												 DOI 10.6019/S-BIAD2339 ReleaseDate 2026-04-01 AttachTo BioImages study-0-pr5o Study Title Spatial in s

evidence_text:
S-BIAD2339 study Spatial in situ transcriptomics of post-transplant HCC recurrence using 10x Genomics Xenium Tomohiko Ikehara 24 2026-04-01 174 True  against post-transplant HCC recurrence. HCC recurrence xenium liver transplantation CC0 https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/legalcode Sample A — primary hepatocellular carcinoma (FFPE, liver)  and Sample B — recurrent hepatocellular carcinoma (FFPE, liver) Homo sapiens (human) Sample S-BIAD2339 Template BioImages.v5 Title Spatial in situ transcriptomics of post-transplant HCC recurrence using 10x Genomics Xenium												 DOI 10.6019/S-BIAD2339 ReleaseDate 2026-04-01 AttachTo BioImages study-0-pr5o Study Title Spatial in situ transcriptomics of post-transplant HCC recurrence using 10x Genomics Xenium												 ReleaseDate 2026-04-01 Description Post-transplant hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) recurrence is a devastating disease with an abysmal prognosis and no established treatment or prevention options. We performed spatial analysis to profile the tumor immune microenvironment in primary HCC at liver transplantation and in recurrent HCC at re-hepatectomy and elucidate the molecular mechanisms that drive recurrence under immunosuppression. This analysis offers potential for developing new therapeutic strategies against post-transplant HCC recurrence. Keywords HCC recurrence Keywords xenium Keywords liver transplantation License CC0 URL https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/legalcode publ-1-v0ps Publication fund-3-sndr Funding bios-4-wj7y Biosample Title Sample A — primary hepatocellular carcinoma (FFPE, liver)  and Sample B — recurrent hepatocellular carcinoma (FFPE, liver) Organism Homo sapiens (human) Description Sample A: primary tumor at liver transplantation
Sample B: recurrent tumor at re-hepatectomy Biological entity tissue; liver; hepatocellular carcinoma (tumor) spec-5-cfvs Specimen Title FFPE section (5 µm) — Sample A and Sample B Sample preparation protocol FFPE block was sectioned at 5 µm and mounted on Xenium slide. 
Dewaxing and pretreatment followed the 10x Genomics Xenium FFPE protocol. 
Sections were hybridized with the Xenium in situ gene expression probe set and processed according to the manufacturer’s instructions. 
An H&E image (10×) was acquired for morphology. imag-6-p9vu Image acquisition Title Xenium acquisition — Sample A and Sample B Imaging instrument 10x Genomics Xenium In Situ Analyzer Image acquisition parameters Pixel size: 0.2125 µm/pixel (full-resolution series; Xenium morphology OME-TIFF is a pyramid)
Channels: Xenium nuclei stain (DAPI fluorescence)
Coordinate units in associated tables: µm; origin: top-left. Imaging method imaging method Imaging method fluorescence microscopy imag-7-og27 Image correlation Title Registration between Xenium coordinates and morphology image — Sample A and Sample B Spatial and temporal alignment Morphology OME-TIFF exported by Xenium Explorer and natively aligned to the 
Xenium transcript coordinate system; single time point. imag-8-unu1 Image analysis Title Xenium decoding and cell segmentation — Sample A and Sample B Image analysis overview Transcript decoding and per-cell quantification were performed using 
10x Genomics Xenium pipeline / Xenium Explorer. 
Outputs include decoded transcripts (transcripts.csv.gz), 
cell/nucleus boundaries (polygons), per-cell attributes (cells.csv.gz), 
and a cell-level feature matrix (HDF5). 
Quality metrics are summarized in metrics_summary.csv. 
Downstream analyses and exact parameters are provided in the Code Ocean capsule. anno-9-y2kf Annotation Title Segmentation and tabular measurements — Sample A and Sample B Annotation Overview Cell and nucleus segmentation from Xenium Explorer;  decoded transcript list; per-cell attributes and counts; gene panel and QC metrics. Annotation Type segmentation_mask Annotation Method Xenium Explorer default model; nucleus expansion 5 µm. File List annotation_files.json stud-10-m3a1 Study Component Name Sample A package, Sample B package Description All files for Sample A and Sample B: morphology OME-TIFF, H&E (optional),  decoded transcripts, cells, boundaries, feature matrix, metrics. File List study_files.json asso-11-42j0 Associations Biosample Sample A — primary hepatocellular carcinoma (FFPE, liver)  and Sample B — recurrent hepatocellular carcinoma (FFPE, liver) Specimen FFPE section (5 µm) — Sample A and Sample B Image acquisition Xenium acquisition — Sample A and Sample B Image correlation Registration between Xenium coordinates and morphology image — Sample A and Sample B Image analysis Xenium decoding and cell segmentation — Sample A and Sample B author-1 Author Name Tomohiko Ikehara E-mail tikehara3@shinshu-u.ac.jp ORCID 0000-0002-1221-567X affiliation o1 True o1 organisation Name Beppu Hospital submission

S-BIAD3618 study Spatial lung niches shape Pseudomonas aeruginosa persistence Duy T. Pham Josephine M. Bryant 1255 2026-06-19 True ) and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), mapping bacterial niches and transcriptional states in situ.
 Cystic fibrosis Xenium CC0 https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/legalcode TB21_0089_RML_D1-S4 Homo sapiens (human) NCBITaxon NCBITaxon_9606 Human Lung section TB21_0089_LUL_A1-S4 Homo S-BIAD3618 Template BioImages.v5 Title Spatial lung niches shape Pseudomonas aeruginosa persistence DOI 10.6019/S-BIAD3618 ReleaseDate 2026-06-19 AttachTo BioImages study-0-fb3i Study Title Spatial lung niches shape Pseudomonas aeruginosa persistence ReleaseDate 2026-06-19 Description Chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa lung infection is a major cause of morbidity and mortality in people with lung disease. Once established, infection is rarely eradicated and often persists despite prolonged antimicrobial therapy, but the mechanisms that support persistence in the human lung remain poorly understood. To define how P. aeruginosa occupies and adapts to diseased lung tissue, we applied host–pathogen spatial transcriptomics to explanted and resected lungs from people with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), mapping bacterial niches and transcriptional states in situ.

evidence_text: S-BIAD3448 study Spatial transcriptomic analysis of sex-biased gene expression in the adult mouse liver James Cleland 115 2026-06-01 True S-BIAD3448 Spatial transcriptomic analysis of sex-biased gene expression in the adult mouse liver 2026-06-01 In this dataset, 10x Xenium was used to profile the expression of 100 sex-biased genes and cell type markers in a fresh-frozen tissue microarray consisting of biopsies from five male S-BIAD3448 Template BioImages.v5 Title Spatial transcriptomic analysis of sex-biased gene expression in the adult mouse liver DOI 10.6019/S-BIAD3448 ReleaseDate 2026-06-01 AttachTo BioImages study-0-obll Study Title Spatial transcriptomic analysis of sex-biased gene expression in the adult mouse liver ReleaseDate 2026-06-01 Description In this dataset, 10x Xenium was used to profile the expression of 100 sex-biased genes and cell type markers in a fresh-frozen tissue microarray consisting of biopsies from five male and five female 12 week old mice. Female biopsies highly express Xist, whereas male biopsies do not. Keywords liver Keywords sexual dimorphism Keywords gene expression Keywords Xenium Acknowledgements We gratefully acknowledge the Single-Cell Open Lab at DKFZ for Xenium data collection and the Center for Model System and Comparative Pathology at the Institute of Pathology of the Heidelberg University Hospital for sectioning. License CC0 URL https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/legalcode Funding statement Acquisition of this dataset was financially supported by DKFZ core funding and a grant from the Wilhelm Sander Stiftung. bios-4-2ox7 Biosample Title Male and female mouse livers Organism Mus musculus domesticus Ontology ncbitaxon TermId NCBITaxon_10092 Description A tissue microarray consisting of biopsies from five male and five female adult livers (12 weeks old, C57BL/6J background). Female biopsies highly express Xist, whereas male biopsies do not. Biological entity Adult mouse liver tissue Experimental variable Sex spec-5-d0d7 Specimen Title Specimen 1 Sample preparation protocol Mouse liver tissue microarray (TMA) cores were cryosectioned at 10 µm thickness as per the Demonstrated Protocol for Xenium In Situ Tissue Preparation for Fresh Frozen Tissues (CG000579, 10x Genomics), mounted on two Xenium slides (PN:1000460, 10x Genomics) and stored at -80°C until further processing. TMA cores were processed according to the Xenium In Situ for Fresh Frozen Tissues- Fixation and Permeabilization protocol (CG000581, 10x Genomics). Briefly, they were thawed at 37°C and fixed with 3.7% formaldehyde (#F8775, Sigma) at room temperature. They were then permeabilized with 1% SDS (#71736, Sigma), incubated with 70% methanol (#32213, Sigma) and washed with 1x PBS. Subsequent steps, including hybridization, post-hybridization washes, ligation, amplification, autofluorescence quenching and nuclei staining were performed using the Xenium Slides and Sample Prep reagents (PN:1000460, 10x Genomics) and following the Xenium In Situ Gene Expression User Guide (CG000582, 10x Genomics). Hybridization was carried out overnight at 50°C, in a Bio-Rad C1000 thermocycler, with a mouse multi-tissue custom panel of 100 genes. imag-6-az48 Image acquisition Title Xenium spatial transcriptomics Imaging instrument 10x Genomics Xenium Analyzer Image acquisition parameters Slides were imaged using the Xenium analyzer following the Xenium Analyzer User Guide (CG000584, 10x Genomics). In the overview scan performed by the instrument, a single region of interest (ROI) was defined per slide, comprising all fields of view (FOVs) containing the TMA cores while excluding the blank areas. Imaging method Xenium stud-10-3dgf Study Component Name Spatial transcriptomic analysis of sex-biased gene expression in the adult mouse liver  Description In this dataset, 10x Xenium was used to profile the expression of 100 sex-biased genes and cell type markers in a fresh-frozen tissue microarray consisting of biopsies from five male and five female 12 week old mice. Wen Zhang Maureen Cowan Anna-Maria Globig Maximilian Heeg David Artis 37 2025-10-24 True  then embedded in paraffin blocks and sectioned at 5um-thickness for spatial transcriptomics. 10x Xenium 10x Xenium 5 cm of the terminal ileum was harvested from TRPV1hM3Dq and littermate control hM3Dqfl/fl mice 24 hours after one dose of 0.1 mg/kg CNO via intraperitoneal injection. Tissues were fixed in 10 S-BIAD2351 Template BioImages.v5 Title A neuronal-epithelial circuit promotes sensory convergence and intestinal immunity. DOI 10.6019/S-BIAD2351 ReleaseDate 2025-10-24 AttachTo BioImages study-0-kpzu Study Title A neuronal-epithelial circuit promotes sensory convergence and intestinal immunity. ReleaseDate 2025-10-24 Description Type 2 inflammation at barrier surfaces is an evolutionarily conserved response that promotes immunity to parasites, allergic inflammation, and tissue repair. Direct sensing of environmental triggers by epithelial cells initiates type 2 inflammation, and signals derived from neurons can modulate immune responses. However, how diverse sensory inputs from epithelial, neuronal, and immune cells are coordinated and integrated remains unclear. Here we identify that TRPV1+ pain-sensing nociceptors co-opt chemosensory epithelial tuft cells to initiate a cascade of tissue responses that drive type 2 inflammation. Chemogenetic silencing or chemical ablation of TRPV1+ nociceptors resulted in a significant reduction in intestinal tuft cells and defective anti-helminth type 2 immunity. In contrast, chemogenetic activation of TRPV1+ nociceptors was associated with remodeling of CGRP+ nerve fibers, significantly elevated levels of CGRP, enhanced tuft cell accumulation, and protective anti-helminth type 2 immunity. Spatial transcriptomic and scRNA-sequencing analysis revealed that nociceptor activation promoted rapid epithelial progenitor cell proliferation and differentiation. Mechanistically, intestinal epithelial cell– and tuft cell–intrinsic expression of CGRP receptor subunits were required for tuft cell responses and type 2 immunity to helminth infection. Together, these results identify sensory convergence of a neuronal-epithelial tuft cell circuit as a critical upstream determinant of type 2 immunity and tissue adaptation.							 Keywords Spatial transcriptomics Keywords Immunology License CC BY 4.0 URL https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode bios-4-g59h Biosample Title Control, Rep 1 Organism Mus musculus (mouse) Biological entity Terminal Ileum bios-12-ux5p-removable Biosample Title Control, Rep 2 Organism Mus musculus (mouse) Biological entity Terminal Ileum bios-13-jnw1-removable Biosample Title Control, Rep 3 Organism Mus musculus (mouse) Biological entity Terminal Ileum bios-14-grlp-removable Biosample Title Trpv1-Cre, Rep 1 Organism Mus musculus (mouse) Biological entity Terminal Ileum bios-15-21pj-removable Biosample Title Trpv1-Cre, Rep 2 Organism Mus musculus (mouse) Biological entity Terminal Ileum bios-16-efny-removable Biosample Title Trpv1-Cre, Rep 3 Organism Mus musculus (mouse) Biological entity Terminal Ileum spec-5-opxy Specimen Title Terminal Ileum Sample preparation protocol 5 cm of the terminal ileum was harvested from TRPV1hM3Dq and littermate control hM3Dqfl/fl mice 24 hours after one dose of 0.1 mg/kg CNO via intraperitoneal injection. Tissues were fixed in 10% neutral-buffered formalin (VWR) for 24 hours at 4 ºC before being transferred to 70% ethanol at 4 ºC. Samples were then embedded in paraffin blocks and sectioned at 5um-thickness for spatial transcriptomics. imag-6-zilu Image acquisition Title 10x Xenium Imaging instrument 10x Xenium Image acquisition parameters 5 cm of the terminal ileum was harvested from TRPV1hM3Dq and littermate control hM3Dqfl/fl mice 24 hours after one dose of 0.1 mg/kg CNO via intraperitoneal injection. Tissues were fixed in 10% neutral-buffered formalin (VWR) for 24 hours at 4 ºC before being transferred to 70% ethanol at 4 ºC. Samples were then embedded in paraffin blocks and sectioned at 5um-thickness for spatial transcriptomics.

Xenium probes were hybridized in situ to target RNA molecules. Each probe contained a unique barcode sequence to enable transcript identification during sequencing-free imaging on the Xenium Analyzer. Xenium analyzer was used to image target RNA positions and cell boundaries. Proprietary software was used to decode and return transcript positions as outputs from the Xenium machine, along with raw images of cell nucleus and boundary stainings.

Xenium slides were processed using the following 10x Genomics user guides: CG000578 (preparation), CG000580 (deparaffinization), CG000749 (hybridization, amplification, staining), and CG000584 (instrument run). Imaging method 10x Xenium stud-10-l8yp Study Component Name A neuronal-epithelial circuit promotes sensory convergence and intestinal immunity. Description Type 2 inflammation at barrier surfaces is an evolutionarily conserved response that promotes immunity to parasites, allergic inflammation, and tissue repair. Direct sensing of environmental triggers by epithelial cells initiates type 2 inflammation, and signals derived from neurons can modulate immune responses. However, how diverse sensory inputs from epithelial, neuronal, and immune cells are coordinated and integrated remains unclear. Here we identify that TRPV1+ pain-sensing nociceptors co-opt chemosensory epithelial tuft cells to initiate a cascade of tissue responses that drive type 2 inflammation. Chemogenetic silencing or chemical ablation of TRPV1+ nociceptors resulted in a significant reduction in intestinal tuft cells and defective anti-helminth type 2 immunity. In contrast, chemogenetic activation of TRPV1+ nociceptors was associated with remodeling of CGRP+ nerve fibers, significantly elevated levels of CGRP, enhanced tuft cell accumulation, and protective anti-helminth type 2 immunity. Spatial transcriptomic and scRNA-sequencing analysis revealed that nociceptor activation promoted rapid epithelial progenitor cell proliferation and differentiation. Mechanistically, intestinal epithelial cell– and tuft cell–intrinsic expression of CGRP receptor subunits were required for tuft cell responses and type 2 immunity to helminth infection. Together, these results identify sensory convergence of a neuronal-epithelial tuft cell circuit as a critical upstream determinant of type 2 immunity and tissue adaptation. S-BSST2361 study SpatialBench: Comparative benchmarking of high-resolution spatial transcriptomics platforms using immune reference tissue Matthew Ritchie (Corresponding Author) Callum Sargeant 3 27 2026-04-08 9 True  0009-0006-8919-608X o2 WEHI - Walter and Eliza Hall Institute of Medical Research https://ror.org/01b6kha49 10X Xenium (1.7.6.0) SpaceRanger Output output directory batch 1 slide 2 Knock Out KO166 10X Xenium (1.7.6.0) SpaceRanger Output output directory batch 1 slide 2 Knock Out KO167 10X Xenium (1.7.6.0 S-BSST2361 Template Default.v2 Title SpatialBench: Comparative benchmarking of high-resolution spatial transcriptomics platforms using immune reference tissue DOI 10.6019/S-BSST2361 ReleaseDate 2026-04-08 study-0-7qy4 Study Title SpatialBench: Comparative benchmarking of high-resolution spatial transcriptomics platforms using immune reference tissue ReleaseDate 2026-04-08 Description The SpatialBench dataset comprises spatial transcriptomics data generated for cross-platform benchmarking of sequencing-based and imaging-based platforms. The study uses fresh-frozen mouse (Mus musculus,  C57/Bl6) spleen tissue from wild-type, control, and knock-out samples, providing a spatially organized immunological tissue with defined structural and transcriptomic differences between conditions. Detailed animal model information provided below. A total of 27 spatial datasets were generated across multiple experimental batches, including 14 from the 10x Genomics Xenium platform (9 unimodal and 5 Multimodal),  9 from Vizgen MERSCOPE, and 4 from the whole transcriptome 10x Genomics Visium HD platform. Xenium and MERSCOPE used targeted gene panels informed by previous SpatialBench Visium dataset (Du, et. al. 2025), including genes relevant to germinal center biology.

Mouse Model:
Mice are a C57/Bl6 Tbx21^fl/flCd23^cre (Knock-Out) strain. Both male and female mice were used for the experiments. Eight to 12-week-old mice were infected intravenously with 5x10^4 P.berghei ANKA pRBCs. Mice were treated with a dose chloroquine (10 mg/kg) and pyrimethamine (10 mg/kg) intraperitoneally at the onset of severe malaria symptoms on day 5 post infection, followed by treatment with drinking water containing chloroquine and pyrimethamine (10 mg/kg) for 7 days. Ashleigh Solano, Raymond K. H. Yip, Changqing Wang, Daniela Amann-Zalcenstein, Pradeep Rajasekhar, Ishrat Zaman, Allan Motyer, Marek Cmero, Yang Xu, Yining Pan, Casey J. A. Anttila, Stephanie Studniberg, Peter F. Hickey, Layla Wang, Callum J. Sargeant, Ling Ling, Yunshun Chen, Ruvimbo Mishi, Lisa J. Ioannidis, Kim Good-Jacobson, Hamish W. King, Kelly L. Rogers, Diana S. Hansen, Rory Bowden, and Matthew E. Ritchie Title SpatialBench: Comparative benchmarking of high-resolution spatial transcriptomics platforms using immune reference tissue Year 2026 Volume Issue Type Issn DOI Abstract Spatial transcriptomics has rapidly expanded with the introduction of multiple high-resolution commercial platforms, yet rigorous cross-platform benchmarking remains limited in scope and biological grounding. We present SpatialBench, an expanded resource for systematic evaluation of emerging technologies in immune tissues. Using spleens from wildtype and B cell specific Tbx21 knockout mice infected with malaria, a model in which loss of Tbet disrupts germinal centre polarization and antibody maturation, we establish a biologically defined ground truth for comparison. We assessed sequencing-based Visium HD, which provides near transcriptome-wide coverage, alongside imaging-based in situ platforms (MERSCOPE, Xenium) that deliver targeted but higher resolution measurements. Visium HD identified spatial gene programs, including a Tbx21 expressing B cell signature and transcriptional profiles distinguishing dark and light zone compartments. Imaging-based platforms offered superior single-cell resolution resulting in cleaner pseudo-bulk differential expression analyses. SpatialBench provides a rigorous and biologically defined framework for evaluating spatial technologies and a reference resource to guide both method development and immunological research. fund-4-woul Funding Agency grant_id author-1 Author Name Matthew Ritchie (Corresponding Author) E-mail mritchie@wehi.edu.au ORCID affiliation o2 True Author Name Callum Sargeant E-mail sargeant.ca@wehi.edu.au ORCID 0009-0006-8919-608X affiliation o2 True o2 organisation Name WEHI - Walter and Eliza Hall Institute of Medical Research RORID https://ror.org/01b6kha49 submission

evidence_text: S-BIAD2208 study Single-cell integration and multi-modal profiling reveals phenotypes and spatial organization of neutrophils in colorectal cancer Alexander Kirchmair Dietmar Rieder Zlatko Trajanoski 2 2251 2025-08-04 True .org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode Single-cell integration and multi-modal profiling reveals phenotypes and spatial organization of neutrophils in colorectal cancer Valentin Marteau, Niloofar Nemati, Kristina Handler https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.08.26.609563 2025 CRC Homo sapiens (human) Tumor cores S-BIAD2208 Template BioImages.v5 Title Single-cell integration and multi-modal profiling reveals phenotypes and spatial organization of neutrophils in colorectal cancer DOI 10.6019/S-BIAD2208 ReleaseDate 2025-08-04 AttachTo BioImages study-0-k2td Study Title Single-cell integration and multi-modal profiling reveals phenotypes and spatial organization of neutrophils in colorectal cancer ReleaseDate 2025-08-04 Description Spatial profiling of colorectal cancer (CRC) tissue samples for the single-cell CRC atlas. This dataset includes Xenium spatial transcriptomics data of 15 patients including tumor cores, invasive margins and adjacent normal tissue sections, and imaging mass cytometry (IMC) data of tumor cores and invasive margins of 29 patients. Keywords Xenium Keywords spatial transcriptomics Keywords imaging mass cytometry Keywords colorectal cancer Keywords neutrophil License CC BY 4.0 URL https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode https://crc.icbi.at/ Description Study overview https://github.com/icbi-lab/crc-atlas Description Code publ-1-9epg Publication Title Single-cell integration and multi-modal profiling reveals phenotypes and spatial organization of neutrophils in colorectal cancer Authors Valentin Marteau, Niloofar Nemati, Kristina Handler DOI https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.08.26.609563 Year 2025 fund-3-hrbu Funding bios-4-kvmg Biosample Title CRC Organism Homo sapiens (human) Description Tumor cores, margins and adjacent normal tissues of FFPE colorectal cancer samples Biological entity Colorectal cancer spec-5-5m5c Specimen Title IMC tissue microarrays Sample preparation protocol Tissue microarrays were generated from intratumoral regions from 27 and tumor margin regions from 29 microsatellite-stable CRC patients. Per sample, one or two 1000x1000 µm regions of interest were ablated, depending on tissue availability. spec-2-h5oc-removable Specimen Title Xenium FFPE tissue sections Sample preparation protocol Sections were prepared according to manufacturer’s instructions (Tissue Preparation Guide Demonstrated Protocol CG000578). 5 µm sections were sectioned from FFPE tissue blocks with a microtome (Epredia, HM355S), floated in a 45˚C water bath, and adhered to Xenium slides (10x Genomics). Samples were baked at 42˚C for 3 hours and stored in a desiccator at room temperature (20°C ± 2°C), overnight, followed by deparaffinization and decrosslinking, according to manufacturer’s instructions (Deparaffinization & Decrosslinking Demonstrated Protocol CG000580). imag-6-xj0p Image acquisition Title IMC Imaging instrument Hyperion Image acquisition parameters Imaging mass cytometry was performed on tissue microarrays from microsatellite-stable CRC patients as previously described (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.isci.2023.108399), using the antibody panel provided in Suppl. Table S3. Imaging method imaging mass spectrometry imag-3-20il-removable Image acquisition Title Xenium Imaging instrument Xenium Analyzer Image acquisition parameters Xenium samples were processed according to manufacturer’s protocols (Xenium In Situ Gene Expression with Morphology-based Cell Segmentation Staining User Guide CG0000749 and Decoding & Imaging, User Guide CG000584). Samples were stained utilizing the predesigned Human Immuno-Oncology panel from 10x genomics (PN 1000654), as they became available from the manufacturer. Slides were stained with a Xenium Cell Segmentation kit according to manufacturer’s instructions (User Guide CG0000749). Briefly, probes of the immuno-oncology panel were hybridized overnight before rolling circle amplification and native protein autofluorescence was reduced with a chemical autofluorescence quencher. Slides were processed on a 10x Xenium Analyzer, with ROIs selected to cover the selected regions. The Xenium Onboard Analysis pipeline v2.0.0 (10x Genomics) was run directly on the instrument for imaging processing, cell segmentation, image registration, decoding, deduplication, and secondary analysis. We generate Xenium 10x data from non-lesional atopic dermatitis skin (n=2) and inflamed atopic dermatitis skin (n=1).
We use these datasets to locate skin fibroblast subtypes identified from scRNA-seq. Keywords human Keywords  skin Keywords fibroblasts License CC0 URL https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/legalcode 10.1101/2024.12.23.629194 Description Type doi publ-1-pkl1 Publication fund-3-87jk Funding bios-4-nyic Biosample Title Inflamed eczema Organism Homo sapiens (human) Biological entity Adult human skin bios-2-xygl-removable Biosample Title Non Inflamed eczema Organism Homo sapiens (human) Biological entity Adult human skin spec-5-uyw5 Specimen Title BK25 Sample preparation protocol Fresh frozen OCT-embedded skin samples were sectioned at 10 µm thickness directly onto the 10X Genomics Xenium slide, kept at -20°C. We then ran the slides through the 10x Genomics Xenium prime in situ gene expression protocol and imaged using the 10X Genomics Xenium Analyzer. This allowed us to probe 5000 genes at subcellular spatial resolution in human skin sections. We include protein staining to facilitate cell segmentation. title: Verifying the spatial structure of precipitation fields from a foundation model of the atmosphere
xenium_reason: No Xenium text or characteristic Xenium files found
evidence_text: Verifying the spatial structure of precipitation fields from a foundation model of the atmosphere <jats:p>Data driven weather models have proven their ability to learn various aspects of the weather prediction problem. While their point-to-point skill has been proven, the precise nature of their errors is not yet fully understood.This contribution takes a first look at the spatial precipitation patterns simulated by the Weather Generator – a foundation model trained on diverse data sources with the goal of learning the underlying behavior of the atmosphere as a whole.  We analyze the correlation structure of the simulated precipitation fields using spatial verification techniques including two-dimensional wavelet transforms. Some attention is paid to the problem of applying these methods to global data on an irregular grid. The results can be compared to observations, reanalysis and potentially other data-driven forecast models.</jats:p>

title: Mixture of Experts with Spatial Routing in a Weather Foundation Model: Early Results from WeatherGenerator
xenium_reason: No Xenium text or characteristic Xenium files found
evidence_text: Mixture of Experts with Spatial Routing in a Weather Foundation Model: Early Results from WeatherGenerator <jats:p>Foundation models have shown strong potential for data-driven weather and climate forecasting by supporting multiple tasks with limited task-specific engineering. The use of architectures that extract maximum value from large, heterogeneous datasets is important in this approach. WeatherGenerator follows this paradigm by learning from diverse observational and reanalysis sources to encode a latent representation of atmospheric dynamics. In this work, we examined the impact of integrating Mixture of Experts (MoE) layers, developed for large language models, into WeatherGenerator, and assessed how MoE can best be incorporated within its decoder architecture.The motivation behind MoE is straightforward: during training, a router learns to assign tokens to specialized experts, allowing different parts of the decoder to focus on distinct spatial regions or physical regimes. We build on this idea by introducing spatially aware routing, in which geographic context is provided to the router, and by evaluating loss-aware routing strategies that favor experts by minimizing local prediction errors.We evaluate four MoE decoder configurations, based on the use of spatial context and loss-aware routing, and compare them against the baseline model. Experiments are conducted using ERA5 reanalysis data, with performance measured using global RMSE and MAE for wind components (u, v), temperature (2t, t850), geopotential height (z500), and specific humidity over three 6-hour autoregressive forecast steps.Across experiments, MoE architectures consistently improve performance for thermodynamic and large-scale variables. In particular, z500 RMSE is reduced by 26–31% at the first forecast step, with spatially aware routing performing best. Near-surface temperature shows a 7% improvement in RMSE and an 11% improvement in MAE when combining spatial and loss-aware routing. These improvements appear early in training within the first few epochs, indicating efficient use of the available data. On the other hand, MoE variants show limited or slightly negative effects at the second and last forecasting step when evaluating wind component variables, while the baseline performance shows similar or better results, especially on the last forecasting step.These preliminary results indicate that MoE provides variable-dependent benefits, with notable improvements for slowly varying, large-scale thermodynamic fields, but less impact on highly dynamic momentum variables. Ongoing work will further assess performance across longer forecast horizons, different climatic regions, and training with multiple datasets from different sources.</jats:p>

title: A Foundation Model for Light Fields: Masked Spatial-Angular Transformer Pretraining (LF-MAE)
xenium_reason: No Xenium text or characteristic Xenium files found
evidence_text: A Foundation Model for Light Fields: Masked Spatial-Angular Transformer Pretraining (LF-MAE) <jats:p>Deep light-field models are commonly trained for a single supervised task such as depth estimation, spatial super-resolution, angular super-resolution, or novel-view synthesis. This narrow training paradigm limits transfer because labeled light-field datasets are relatively small, task-specific, and expensive to acquire. We propose LF-MAE (light field masked autoencoders), a self-supervised masked autoencoding framework that learns reusable 4D spatial-angular representations from unlabeled light fields. LF-MAE divides each sub-aperture image into spatial patches, attaches separable angular and spatial positional embeddings, hides a large subset of tokens, and trains an asymmetric transformer encoder-decoder to reconstruct the missing light-field content. Unlike ordinary image MAE (masked autoencoders) pretraining, LF-MAE introduces light-field-specific masking modes: random spatial-angular patch masking, full angular-view masking, EPI-line masking, disparity-band masking, and refocus-plane masking. These objectives force the model to infer parallax, occlusion, disparity slope, and refocusing cues rather than merely local image texture. The resulting encoder can be fine-tuned for depth, spatial or angular super-resolution, segmentation, material recognition, and active acquisition.</jats:p>

title: Odon: An ultra-fast viewer for spatial proteomics
xenium_reason: Xenium is mentioned without evidence of dataset generation or reuse
evidence_text:
Odon: An ultra-fast viewer for spatial proteomics <jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>Multiplexed spatial proteomics and spatial transcriptomics generate large, high-dimensional imaging datasets that are challenging to visualize efficiently, particularly at whole-slide and cohort scale. Visualization is an essential step for rapid detection of staining artefacts, such as protein aggregates or non-specific staining. Here, we present Odon, a native Rust desktop viewer designed for rapid, interactive exploration of multiplex imaging data on a standard laptop. Odon is primarily built around the OME-Zarr imaging format, and supports annotations via GeoJSON and GeoParquet, with secondary support for SpatialData, Xenium containers, and TIFF. Data can be stored locally or streamed directly from HTTP or S3-compatible object storage using viewport-driven tile loading. Odon incorporates a highly optimized rendering engine that substantially outperforms existing viewers. In benchmarking, Odon loaded a 32 GB, 36-plex whole-slide OME-Zarr image in under 1 second, compared with 10.14 seconds for QuPath and 35 seconds for Napari. Its GPU-based compositing pipeline also enables smooth rendering and interaction with more than 1,000,000 segmented cells, exceeding the practical limits of many existing tools. Odon further supports integrated visual analytics, including live thresholding and cell selection, and a mosaic mode for simultaneous viewing of hundreds of regions of interest in cohort and tissue microarray studies. Together, these features establish Odon as a high-performance platform for scalable visualization of spatial proteomics data.</jats:p>

title: MetaboFM: A Foundation Model for Spatial Metabolomics
xenium_reason: No Xenium text or characteristic Xenium files found
evidence_text:
MetaboFM: A Foundation Model for Spatial Metabolomics <jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) provides molecularly resolved maps of metabolites and lipids across tissues, yet the lack of large-scale, unified representation learning frameworks limits its potential for generalization and downstream analysis. Here, we introduce MetaboFM, a foundation model for spatial metabolomics that consolidates thousands of public MSI datasets into standardized spatial–spectral tensors and extracts transferable embeddings using pretrained Vision Transformers. We curated and standardized around 4000 publicly available MSI datasets from the METASPACE repository, spanning multiple organisms, tissue types, ionization modes, and instruments. Across six metadata prediction tasks—encompassing organism, ionization polarity, tissue type, condition, analyzer type, and ionization source embeddings from pretrained MetaboFM encoders achieved a mean macro–F1 of 0.74 and accuracy of 0.80 with linear probes, demonstrating substantially higher discriminative power than classical principal component analysis (PCA) or randomly initialized baselines by over 20 percentage points. To interpret the learned representations, we mapped embedding directions back to the
                  <jats:italic>m/z</jats:italic>
                  domain, revealing distinct spectral regions that drive class separation across tissues, conditions, and ionization sources. A multimodal visual question answering (VQA) extension further links MSI embeddings with natural-language queries through a cross-attention fusion module, attaining an average macro–F1 of 0.61 ± 0.05 across tasks. Finally, an interactive Gradio interface enables users to visualize MSI patches and query sample metadata in free-form language. Together, MetaboFM establishes a scalable foundation model paradigm for MSI, unifying representation learning, spectral interpretability, and multimodal interaction within a single framework for spatial metabolomics.
                </jats:p>

evidence_text:
A Multimodal Foundation Model for Discovering Genetic Associations with Brain Imaging Phenotypes <jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Due to the intricate etiology of neurological disorders, finding interpretable associations between multi-omics features can be challenging using standard approaches. We propose
                  <jats:monospace>COMICAL</jats:monospace>
                  , a contrastive learning approach leveraging multi-omics data to generate associations between genetic markers and brain imaging-derived phenotypes.
                  <jats:monospace>COMICAL</jats:monospace>
                  jointly learns omic representations utilizing transformer-based encoders with custom tokenizers. Our modality-agnostic approach uniquely identi-fies many-to-many associations via self-supervised learning schemes and cross-modal attention encoders.
                  <jats:monospace>COMICAL</jats:monospace>
                  discovered several significant associations between genetic markers and imaging-derived phenotypes for a variety of neurological disorders in the UK Biobank as well as predicting across diseases and unseen clinical outcomes from the learned representations. Source code of
                  <jats:monospace>COMICAL</jats:monospace>
                  along with pre-trained weights, enabling transfer learning is available at
                  <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="https://github.com/IBM/comical">https://github.com/IBM/comical</jats:ext-link>
                  .
                </jats:p>

title: GenBrain: A Generative Foundation Model of Multimodal Brain Imaging
xenium_reason: No Xenium text or characteristic Xenium files found
evidence_text:
GenBrain: A Generative Foundation Model of Multimodal Brain Imaging <jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>Neuroimaging faces a reproducibility crisis, where studies on small, heterogeneous datasets produce unreliable brain-wide associations and AI models that fail to generalize. To address this, we introduce GenBrain, a generative foundation model pretrained on approximately 1.2 million 3D scans from over 44,000 individuals across 34 imaging modalities to learn a population prior of brain structure and function. Crucially, GenBrain enables rapid, data-efficient adaptation, allowing any targeted study to generate biologically valid synthetic cohorts, conditioned on demographics, disease status, or other modalities, to augment statistical power and enhance generalizability. We demonstrate GenBrain’s transformative utility across 81 independent datasets spanning diverse populations, protocols, and clinical conditions. For image-level tasks, it achieves state-of-the-art performance in image enhancement and cross-modality synthesis while preserving subject-specific neurobiology. In population neuroscience, synthetic cohorts from GenBrain stabilize effect-size estimates and significantly improve the reproducibility of brain-wide association studies. For clinical AI, disease-specific fine-tuning of GenBrain substantially boosts the cross-site generalizability of prediction models. Finally, we prove its direct translational value when adapted to unseen modality and scarce clinical stroke data. GenBrain significantly improves predictions of acute stroke severity and chronic aphasia, demonstrating actionable utility under extreme data scarcity. By empowering small-scale studies with large-scale population priors, GenBrain provides a unified framework for more reproducible and clinically generalizable neuroimaging analysis.</jats:p>

title: A Multimodal Foundation Model Framework for Globally-Coupled Core-Native Cloud Computing Networks and Chiplet Architecture Co-Design
publisher: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
xenium_reason: No Xenium text or characteristic Xenium files found
evidence_text:
A Multimodal Foundation Model Framework for Globally-Coupled Core-Native Cloud Computing Networks and Chiplet Architecture Co-Design Abstract

The convergence of cloud-native computing, chiplet-based architectures, and multimodal foundation models presents a transformative opportunity to reimagine global-scale AI infrastructure. However, existing frameworks fail to jointly optimize across network topology, compute heterogeneity, and model sparsity under energy and latency constraints. Here we present GlobeCore-ChipNet, a multimodal foundation model framework that co-designs globally-coupled core-native cloud networks and chiplet architectures. GlobeCore-ChipNet integrates a graph-transformer hybrid encoder to model cross-layer dependencies, a spatio-temporal tokenizer for workload dynamics, and a differentiable co-simulator for hardware–software co-exploration. Trained on 1.2 million hours of real workload traces from 42 hyperscale data centers and 1,800 chiplet configurations, our framework achieves 34.7 % average energy-delay product (EDP) reduction over state-of-the-art baselines. Zero-shot generalization to unseen chiplet topologies exhibits <3.1 % error. We demonstrate feasibility via a 5 nm prototype tape-out integrating 3D-stacked chiplets and silicon photonics interconnects. Our results suggest that GlobeCore-ChipNet enables scalable, energy-efficient, and reconfigurable AI infrastructure for next-generation cloud-native systems.

GenBrain: A Generative Foundation Model of Multimodal Brain Imaging Abstract

Neuroimaging faces a reproducibility crisis, where studies on small, heterogeneous datasets produce unreliable brain-wide associations and AI models that fail to generalize. To address this, we introduce GenBrain, a generative foundation model pretrained on approximately 1.2 million 3D scans from over 44,000 individuals across 34 imaging modalities to learn a population prior of brain structure and function. Crucially, GenBrain enables rapid, data-efficient adaptation, allowing any targeted study to generate biologically valid synthetic cohorts, conditioned on demographics, disease status, or other modalities, to augment statistical power and enhance generalizability. We demonstrate GenBrain’s transformative utility across 81 independent datasets spanning diverse populations, protocols, and clinical conditions. For image-level tasks, it achieves state-of-the-art performance in image enhancement and cross-modality synthesis while preserving subject-specific neurobiology. In population neuroscience, synthetic cohorts from GenBrain stabilize effect-size estimates and significantly improve the reproducibility of brain-wide association studies. For clinical AI, disease-specific fine-tuning of GenBrain substantially boosts the cross-site generalizability of prediction models. Finally, we prove its direct translational value when adapted to unseen modality and scarce clinical stroke data. GenBrain significantly improves predictions of acute stroke severity and chronic aphasia, demonstrating actionable utility under extreme data scarcity. By empowering small-scale studies with large-scale population priors, GenBrain provides a unified framework for more reproducible and clinically generalizable neuroimaging analysis.

title: Human Modalities in Human–Robot Interaction: A Review from Multimodal and Foundation Model Perspectives
publisher: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE)
xenium_reason: No Xenium text or characteristic Xenium files found
evidence_text:
Human Modalities in Human–Robot Interaction: A Review from Multimodal and Foundation Model Perspectives <jats:p>The field of human-robot interaction (HRI) has experienced remarkable
growth, fueled by the increasing integration of robotic technologies
into various aspects of human life. As robots become more ubiquitous,
the need for effective and natural communication between humans and
robots is paramount. Human modalities, which encompass the diverse ways
in which humans perceive, interpret, and express information, play a
crucial role in enabling seamless and efficient HRI. This review
explores the state-of-the-art in core human modalities, analyzing their
strengths and limitations. Furthermore, this paper explores the realm of
multimodal interaction, characterized by the concurrent employment of
various modalities to enrich and fortify the interaction process. The
discussion extends to the potentialities and challenges inherent in
multimodal HRI. Additionally, we examine how foundation models
revolutionize the semantic interpretation and fusion of human
modalities.</jats:p>

title: LunarFM: A Multimodal Foundation Model for Lunar Science
xenium_reason: No Xenium text or characteristic Xenium files found
evidence_text:
LunarFM: A Multimodal Foundation Model for Lunar Science

LunarFM is a multimodal foundation model for lunar geospatial analysis and resource prospecting. Built on a multi-task masked autoencoder (MultiMAE) architecture, it integrates six orbital instrument modalities — including elevation, optical imagery, gravity anomalies, and spectral data — into a unified representation of the lunar surface. LunarFM enables downstream tasks such as mineral concentration prediction, geological mapping, crater detection, and similarity-based prospecting, and provides a natural language interface for querying lunar geology via retrieval-augmented generation.

title: Abstract MP05: A Preliminary 10X Genomics Xenium Profiler Spatial Transcriptomics Map of Renin-Angiotensin System Genes in the Mouse Subfornical Organ
xenium_reason: New profiling/generation with Xenium is stated
evidence_text: Abstract MP05: A Preliminary 10X Genomics Xenium Profiler Spatial Transcriptomics Map of Renin-Angiotensin System Genes in the Mouse Subfornical Organ <jats:p>The subfornical organ (SFO) is a brain region rich in angiotensin-II (ANG) AT1 receptors (AT1R) which regulates water and salt intake and communicates with other hypothalamic nuclei to regulate blood pressure (BP). Activation of the classical G protein-mediated AT1R signaling in the brain induces pressor and natri-dipsogenic responses. TRV027, a synthetic AT1R β-arrestin-biased agonist, decreases BP and increases saline avoidance. We examined the spatial transcriptomic makeup of the SFO to balanced or biased AT1R activation using 10X Genomics Xenium. We treated C57BL/6J mice with either aCSF, ANG (0.64 µg/h), or TRV027 (0.56 µg/h) through continuous ICV infusion for 11-days. Water intake was monitored on days 6-11. ANG caused an increase in water intake compared to aCSF or TRV027 groups (7.0±2.0, 3.0±0.2, and 1.9±0.6 mL/day, respectively, n=4, p<0.05). On day 11, brains were perfused, collected, and fixed. Subsequently, 5 µm paraffin-embedded coronal sections were mounted on Xenium slides and tested with the 10X mouse brain set of 247 genes plus 100 custom add-on genes including all RAS genes. A third of the cells expressed AT1R, and the pattern of AT1R-expressing cells Xenium-detected was qualitatively similar to NZ44 mice, which express GFP under the control of the AT1R locus. Expression of the prorenin receptor (<jats:italic toggle="yes">Atp6ap2</jats:italic>) was abundant throughout the SFO. A few cells in the SFO and more prominently in the adjacent blood vessels were angiotensinogen positive. Single cells were identified that expressed the ANG type 2 receptors (AT2R). Expression of Mas receptor was undetectable. Most of the AT1R-expressing cells were excitatory glutamatergic neurons (expressing <jats:italic toggle="yes">Slc17a6</jats:italic>, VGLUT2) but a few were inhibitory GABAergic neurons co-expressing <jats:italic toggle="yes">Slc32a1</jats:italic> (VGAT). Each section had cells classified as astrocytes, neurons, ependymal cells, and immune cells (e.g., microglia or macrophages). Increased numbers of immune cells were detected in the SFO of ANG-treated mice. Interestingly, although there was no evidence for expression of renin in the SFO, renin-expressing cells were identified in the choroid plexus which is consistent with highly sensitive in situ RNA hybridization data. Additional qualitative and quantitative analyses are ongoing to compare gene expression patterns in AT1R-positive cell types. Future studies will identify gene expression patterns in SFO neurons with connectivity to other brain regions controlling water/salt intake and BP.</jats:p>
